turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: We'll hear argument first this morning in Case 15簫375, Supap Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons. Mr. Rosenkranz.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: When Congress modified the American Rule in the Copyright Act, it was not just trying to punish those who took unreasonable positions. It wanted to encourage parties to advance important principles even where the other side's arguments are good; indeed, I would say, especially where the other side's arguments are good. When a defendant is trying to decide whether to fight for a principle, the availability of attorneys' fees can make all the difference in that decision, and in turn can make all the difference in whether the public's rights are vindicated. The Second Circuit's standard flouts. The plain language of the statute undermines Congress's goal and is consistent with this Court's opinion in Fogerty. It does nothing to encourage a defendant who has a good defense but is facing off against a powerful adversary armed with a reasonable position. That encouragement has not happened once in the last 15 years. That's not once in 187 cases decide 簫簫 decided under the Second Circuit's Matthew Bender rubric, and will never happen anywhere outside the Second Circuit.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Rosenkranz, if Kirtsaeng had lost this case 簫簫 if he had lost this case, should fees have been awarded to Wiley, given the significance of this decision? I mean, it's an important decision. It needs both sides to be aired before the Court. So suppose he had lost and Wiley won, would Wiley be entitled to attorneys' fees?

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Wiley would have an argument, Your Honor, certainly on one of the factors that we have suggested, which is it would say we won an important case. It didn't win it against the 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: It would have had more than that. It would have had circuit precedent. It could have been woefully infringing, correct?

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Not 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: If I were going to bet, I would say yes to that question. Wouldn't you?

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: I'm sorry. Our client would have been 簫簫 was already found to have woefully infringed.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: So our client had a lot already weighing against him. But just to get back to finish the answer to Justice Ginsburg's question, but there would have been other factors. The Court would have 簫簫 the district court would have evaluated what the incentives for both sides were. So Wiley would have had an enormous economic incentive to advance its position. Kirtsaeng would have had much less of an incentive to do anything other than to cave.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Why? It was a lucrative business he was engaged in.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Your Honor, he was a student who had this side business who was making just a few dollars per book. I mean, it was a 簫簫 it was a large volume, but the 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Several hundred thousand.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Several hundred thousand dollars in revenues, Your Honor, but not in profit. And as to Wiley 簫簫 as to the books of Wiley that he sold, it was $37,000, Your Honors, for which he was hit with a $600,000 judgment, which also would have been considered by the district court. It would have thought 簫簫 it would have asked itself: Is this fair? But the problem with the Second Circuit's position is that it prejudges in every case there is going to be substantial weight on the reasonableness.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Rosenkranz, just to continue on with what Justice Ginsburg was asking you. As an ex簫post matter, you have a great David versus Goliath story to tell. But as an ex簫ante matter, I wonder if the rule that you suggest is not going to harm the Kirtsaengs of the world. And you know, you might take these couple things into account: That the Kirtsaengs of the world will probably think that the 簫簫 that they are spending less on their lawyers than the John Wileys of the world. And that they're also more risk averse, because they have less money. So, you know, given those two factors, doesn't your rule, actually as an ex簫ante matter, cut against the Kirtsaengs of the world?

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Your Honor, the answer is no, and for this reason. The Kirtsaengs of the world, when they are facing off against a John Wiley, the first questions on their minds before they ever think about attorneys' fees being awarded against them is how am I going to pay for this? And critical answer to that question will be in many circumstances, there's the availability of attorneys' fees 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Well, but that's only one side of the question. I mean, what you're doing is you're upping the stakes generally so that Kirtsaeng is going to know, well, it's true I might be able to get my fees back; but at the same time, I run the risk of having to pay John Wiley's fees. And John Wiley, as I said, is probably going to be paying its lawyers more than I'm going to be paying mine. And I'm very risk簫sensitive as a Kirtsaeng type. So that's a huge deal for me to increase my stakes that much.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Your Honor, understood. That will be part of the calculus. Now, in the Ninth Circuit where the test that we are suggesting predominates, and in every circuit that doesn't accept the Second Circuit's position, which is to say every other circuit, there is not a dearth of copyright litigation. People in Kirtsaeng's position are fighting ahead. And why is that? It's because district courts have been entrusted with making reasonable judgments and asking the question: If a defendant is in exactly this position in the next case, what are the incentives that will appropriately incentivize both the plaintiff to push on and the defendant.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: I thought you told 簫簫 I thought you told us that there haven't been any awards under the 簫簫 maybe I misunderstood 簫簫 the Second Circuit test in 15 years. Or what was the point you were making at the beginning, 187 cases, 15 years?

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: 178 cases, Your Honor, that were decided under the Second Circuit's Matthew Bender standard, not once did any defendant 簫簫 defendant, that is, ever prevail in receiving attorneys' fees except where the plaintiff 簫簫

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: 簫簫 has been 簫簫 has engaged in unreasonable 簫簫

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Unreasonable.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: 簫簫 conduct.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: But I thought your point 簫簫 your point now is that the defendants in copyright cases still show up.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: No, Your Honor. I'm talking about the rest of the world. I'm talking about the Ninth Circuit. The Court: Okay.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: So in the Ninth Circuit, plaintiffs show up, starving artists show up, and starving artists defend.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: And they don't in the Second Circuit?

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Then the truth is there are very few starving artists in any copyright litigation. So, yes, they may show up, but they won't be able to pay for their own lawyer. And so 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Do you suggest that one of the solutions to that problem is for the district court to take into account the relative financial resources of the parties; is that correct?

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, as 簫簫 as one of the factors. So what the district court should do in every case, in addition to all of the footnote 19 factors, is to ask itself: If a 簫簫 if a party in the same position reads the precedents or their lawyers read the precedents, what lessons will they glean from them? And one of the things a district court should be analyzing in every case looking backwards is: What would have been the right economic incentives for this plaintiff and this defendant?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Is there anything 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: Have we ever said in a 簫簫 have we ever said that the availability of attorneys' fees is dependent on the financial resources of the party?

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Your Honor, it's 簫簫 it's certainly 簫簫 it has long been a part of the standard that courts have consistently applied in attorneys' fees cases. This Court said in Fogerty you've got to evaluate the incentives, and the incentives run both ways. A John Wiley did not need attorneys' fees in order to proceed, and was not worried about attorneys' fees, in order to protect its interest in a $1.8簫billion簫a簫year business for which, on this particular issue, it stood to gain hundreds of millions of dollars. It wasn't worried about whether it would have to pay Sam Israel's $125,000 in fees. A Kirtsaeng, on the other hand, would have been worried about that under 簫簫 under any standard. And in a standard where he stands to gain attorneys' fees for defending, that would have been an important incentive to 簫簫 to encourage him to soldier on 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Rosenkranz 簫簫

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: 簫簫 under 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: 簫簫 it does sound like, as Justice Kagan put it, that your rule is if David faces Goliath and David wins, David gets fees no matter how reasonable Goliath's position was. That's what it seems to come down to.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Your Honor, it doesn't. On that factor of 簫簫 I mean, there are easily six factors a court should be considering. On that factor, a David is better positioned than a Goliath to make the argument that he should get fees. But there's a lot more to it. There's, what else does the defendant stand to gain? What does the plaintiff stand to gain? What were the motivations of the parties? One of the factors that the district court said was completely irrelevant was, what was the significance of the win to the public? So all of 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: So one thing that concerns me about a test like that, Mr. Rosenkranz, is it's very hard for people to make judgments ex ante and to figure out what their chances are. It's very hard to predict. And if you're concerned about the Kirtsaengs of the world, the Davids in these kinds of suits, what you might want is a pretty clear safe harbor. In other words, if I'm taking a reasonable position, I'm not going to be stuck with the other side's fees, which are likely to dwarf my own. And that's something that somebody can predict, as opposed to this 22簫factor test, which it's like I just don't know how this is going to come out, and I might well be stuck with John Wiley's fees, depending on what judge I draw and, oh, a number of other things that I don't have any control over.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Understood, Your Honor. So what the 簫簫 what the Congress did was to select a standard based upon the totality of the circumstances. It affirmatively rejected the standard that sat there in the Patent Act and in the Lanham Act that was based on exactly the clear line that you've described, Your Honor, exceptional circumstances. To this day, Wiley has not explained how its test is any different from the one that Congress adopted. It's exactly what Justice Kennedy 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: What is 簫簫 what about 簫簫 I suppose I start this thinking district judges do have a job. In part of that job, they do things that we would do worse, not better. And one of the things that they know is the case in front of them. And what we said in the 簫簫 our case was: It's up to them as long as they act reasonably. So you started with a factor, which I guess they could have taken into account. Nothing in our law prohibits it. In fact, it encourages it. So what's the problem? What is it? Are you saying we should, in fact, change what we said in Fogerty? In my own mind, I don't know how to do it.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: No, Your Honor. I 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: All right. So you don't want to believe that. Okay. So you want case簫specific correction of what you believe is a failure to apply to realize what Fogerty meant. Is that the idea?

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. So let me be more precise. I've already said one of the things that is wrong with the Matthew Bender standard, that it is atextual. A second thing, directly to the question on Fogerty, is that Fogerty says, quote, "defendants who seek to advance meritorious copyright defenses should be encouraged to litigate them. There" is nothing in Matthew Bender that encourages a defender to litigate.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: No, but you want us case簫specific or you want us to say something? I mean, one 簫簫 one whole 簫簫 one result would be the Second Circuit seems not to have taken account of what we said, which is that all considerations 簫簫 all considerations that are consistent with the purposes of the Copyright Act can be relevant. It depends on the case, period.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Absolutely, Your Honor. Although, I 簫簫 I would add a little bit more. I 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: You wanted this other thing, which I don't know how to do. The other thing is whether you really advance the law. I mean, maybe at the time, Marbury v. Madison was viewed by many people as being just about an appointment, and it didn't really advance the law.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Your Honor 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Maybe others thought it did. I don't know. How do we know which advances the law?

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Your Honor, district courts know what cases are advancing the law and what cases are more specific 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Mr. Rosenkranz, I 簫簫 I think that Justice Breyer is getting to something. Put on a hat that doesn't want to win outright. Okay? Put on a hat where we're trying to announce a rule. And I'm sympathetic to your argument that the Second Circuit rule obviously stacks everything in favor of a winning plaintiff, publish a winning copyright holder because 80 percent are now winning, if not more. In the Second Circuit, it's almost 89 percent. It means, when you're talking about the reasonableness of a 簫簫 a winning party's position, in most cases the copyright holder has a reasonable position. So 簫簫 and the defendant, by losing, tends to have an unreasonable position. I looked at what happened after Fogerty, and the 簫簫 after Fogerty and Fantasy, that's where the Ninth Circuit tests began. And the Court there said there's different incentives for plaintiffs and defendants. You can't make the reasonableness of the position the centerpiece for prevailing defendants, or otherwise, they're never going to get fees, or hardly ever. So going back to Justice Breyer's question, how do we articulate what the Second Circuit is doing wrong without necessarily endorsing all of the factors of the Ninth Circuit?

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Understood, Your Honor. The simple answer is: What the Second Circuit did was to pick one factor out of a jumble of possible factors and say, this will be the one that gets substantial weight. And what the district courts do with that is then hold up that one factor and ask, is there anything that outweighs that factor when, in the context of a particular case, that might not be the most important factor? But I see the Court wrestling with 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So the test would be, it's okay to have it one among others, but not a presumption that says that's always going to entitle you to defend against an award or to win an award.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Agreed. And what Matthew Bender does is essentially to announce a presumption. But I see the Court wrestling with 簫簫 with how to articulate a test. I can articulate the test in four sentences in a way the district courts can administer. So our rule is that a district court should consider the totality of the circumstances, including all of the Fogerty factors, and ask itself, would a fee award here advance the purposes of the Copyright Act? Is this the sort of case in which, if the same scenario were to present itself again, the availability of fees would create the right litigation centives 簫簫 incentives, that is, for both parties with the right result for the public? The district court should 簫簫 should consider each of the Fogerty factors and do it through the lens of the purposes of the Copyright Act. It should also consider the significance and nature of the win and the litigation incentives on both sides of the deed, including any disparity in resources. That is 簫簫 if 簫簫 if this Court were to say those four sentences, district courts would have a lot of guidance. And if this Court were to say, Fogerty, too, actually does it right 簫簫 it didn't just consider litigation incentives on one side. It considered the litigation incentives for the plaintiffs.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: But it seems to me that you 簫簫 a 簫簫 a party can advance the law and a 簫簫 a case can advance the law by insisting on principled, consistent application of settled principles. That's an advancing of the law. I 簫簫 I can see the excitement about granting fees if there's some 簫簫 some breakthrough, something we've never thought about. On the other hand, there's something that's commendable about applying the law consistently, routinely in regular cases. That advances the law.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Of 簫簫 of course, it does. And 簫簫 and in the right case, that can support a fees award. For example, the only way to define fair use is case簫by簫case, accretively where a common law develops. Someone who advances a fair use defense and wins ought to be at least within range of a copyright 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Rosenkranz, can we go back to your test now? Would you say we'll make it something district courts can understand and readily apply? So take my question. Kirtsaeng loses and Wiley win 簫簫 wins. Apply your four簫sentence test, and tell me whether Wiley gets fees.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: The answer will depend on a district court's specific balancing, but I'll do the balance that I would do 簫簫 that I would argue to the district court. I would say, well, first, Wiley did win something really big here. Although it wasn't against huge headwinds, the precedent was in its favor. Good for Wiley. It gets credit for that. But Wiley had every incentive to protect hundreds of millions of dollars. It didn't need attorneys' fees in order to incentivize it. Kirtsaeng also did something important. It stood up 簫簫 he stood up. He had a reasonable position. That counts. It's not dispositive, but it counts. And what good would it have 簫簫 would it have future litigants 簫簫 would it do for future litigants to hit this poor guy who is a student with attorneys' fees when he's already got a $600,000 judgment against him? That would be my advocacy. I could see a district court adopting it. I could see a district court going the other way. But a common law will emerge, as it has in the Ninth Circuit. Impecunious defendants are not shying away from fighting a big copyright Goliath if they can afford the lawsuit that is to pay their own attorneys' fees on this rampant copyright litigation in the Ninth Circuit, and in the other circuits that don't start with a presumption against attorneys' fees if the other side was reasonable.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Can I 簫簫 can I ask, Mr. Rosenkranz, one of the things that confused me about this case and about, actually, both sides' arguments, I 簫簫 I don't really understand why it is that the fees are awarded in such a high percentage of the cases, both in the Second Circuit and elsewhere. I mean, the Second Circuit says that its 簫簫 the first簫among簫equal factors is the reasonableness, but it awards fees in more 簫簫 way more than half the cases. Is it that so many of the cases are utterly frivolous?

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: So, Your Honor, the answer has two parts: There is a different test for defendants than for plaintiffs, notwithstanding what you read in Matthew Bender. So the plaintiff 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Before 簫簫 before you talk to me about the 簫簫 the way this might or might not be pro簫plaintiff or pro簫defendant, just why are they all so high?

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Well 簫簫 well, the numbers are so high for plaintiffs because plaintiffs are generally getting copyright fees, even if the other side was perfectly reasonable, because there's a blameworthiness element to it. So this 簫簫 you know, this so簫and簫so infringer should be smacked with a 簫簫 with attorneys' fees. And by the way, there's often willfulness, as there was in this case, because a legal defense is no defense. On the defendant's side 簫簫 and the Second Circuit is certainly not more than half. It's around half. And that's only where the other side has behaved unreasonably, either in its litigation position, that is, the validity of its claim, or in its litigation position's sort of aggressive tactics. Those are the only circumstances in 178 cases in which defendants have ever had copyright fees awarded in their favor. And this is something that 簫簫 that we have yet to hear 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Well, you know, it's 簫簫 I have no idea why. I could speculate. One problem is that a lot of college students think they should listen to all the music they want and they don't pay any copyright. That would be outrageous, right? That's 簫簫 I read that in the papers and other places as a problem.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Sure 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Maybe, from time to time, the copyright owners feel that, you know, my employees have to pay for gasoline at Exxon; why should Exxon's employees take all my works for free?

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Sure, Your Honor. And that's why 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Okay. So we have no idea why 簫簫 at least from these briefs, I have no idea why the copyright numbers come out the way they do on fees; therefore, I'm thinking, quite honestly, it's going to vary from case to case. I understand appellate lawyers love to create standards. I do not have that love at this moment.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Your Honor, I 簫簫 I 簫簫 I understand the lack of love. If that's what the 簫簫 what is motivating the Court, it should reject Matthew Bender. That's 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: It might be you say we go back and say have they taken what used to be an all簫factors test, and have they in fact said 簫簫 and then we could all go home. We say 簫簫 we say what they've done here is they've said we're never going to 簫簫 or hardly ever going to take a favorable account of having clarified the law. And in your view what we should say is, well, don't say never. Don't lay down a standard. There are too many different kinds of cases. Beware of trying to 簫簫 is that 簫簫 that's what you want us to. That's it.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Yes. Yes. And I would also encourage the Court to say when 簫簫 when a district court is evaluating each factor, it should be thinking to itself, what's the purpose 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: You have added a couple of standards. Your first two sentences were fine. They said roughly what 簫簫 what we've been talking about. And then you had two later sentences which I began to think, hey, that's going to be a little tough to apply.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Your Honor, my later sentences 簫簫 you're 簫簫 you are right, were two sentences. One is: Think of the Fogerty factors in Footnote 9 and do it through the lens of the purposes of the Copyright Act. And the second is: By the way, Fogerty was about more than footnote 19. It was about incentivizing each side correctly. Don't forget about that. So consider the incentives on both sides.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: The problem with that is that different judges are going to have very different views about what will further the purposes of the Copyright Act. Don't you think both the Second Circuit and the Ninth Circuit 簫簫 I'm sorry 簫簫 the Seventh Circuit think that their rules are the rules that best further the purposes of the Copyright Act?

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. And that's why both of them are wrong. I would love the Court to adopt the Seventh Circuit's standard, but it too prejudges 簫簫 in every case it says here's what will further the purposes of the 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: The 簫簫 the footnote does not say further the purposes of the Act. What the footnote says is, Judge, when you award these fees, be certain that you are faithful to the purposes of the Act. And that's very different. It means don't do something that's going to undermine the Act, as opposed to sitting there and figuring out whether the basic purpose of the Act is to what extent to encourage authors at the expense of the readers, or the expense of those people who do not want to undergo huge transaction costs getting ahold of dead authors, all right? So we have several different conflicting purposes. You want to bring them in when you say "further," and the court did not bring them in because it said "are faithful to." You don't have to answer that.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: No, your Honor. But my answer is 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: 簫簫 very short. Yes, I agree. I accept what I think is a friendly amendment to my 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: 簫簫 description of Fogerty. Thank you Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Smith.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This is a case, I submit, where the lower courts did everything right. As courts have been doing in copyright cases for more than a century, the district court focused first on the question of whether or not the losing party, here Wiley, had 簫簫 had taken an unreasonable litigation position on the law or on the facts, and concluded that it obviously had not. And then it looked at all the other potentially relevant factors, including all of the factors that had been suggested by Kirtsaeng's counsel, and concluded that 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Except there is 簫簫 Mr. Smith, I'm trying by what's happening in the Second Circuit. I've actually, with the help of the library, looked at cases in the Second and the Ninth Circuit for the last three years, and this is what I'm coming out with: In the Ninth Circuit, prevailing defendants have received fees 20 times and lost 17 times, about 50 percent. In the Second circuit, prevailing defendants have received fees three times and lost 16 times. That's a huge difference. If I look at what's happening with prevailing plaintiffs, in the Second Circuit, prevailing plaintiffs awarded fees 23 times, and prevailing plaintiffs denied fees seven. In the Ninth, prevailing plaintiffs got it 46 times and denied fees twice. Prevailing plaintiffs are winning everywhere in extraordinary numbers. And in both circuits, prevailing defendants are not winning hardly at all. At best, 50 percent in the Ninth Circuit. So does it say something that somehow, prevailing 簫簫 that this presumption that the Second Circuit is giving is unfair to defendants and to the purposes of the copyright law?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: No, your Honor. First of all, I would note that Mr. Kirtsaeng's own attorneys have reported that if a 簫簫 a more complete study of the Second Circuit record says that defendants are winning them 50 percent of the time. The 簫簫

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Sorry. Five簫oh, 50?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. That's in their reply brief.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But that was a study from 2000, correct?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: No, no, no. They did 簫簫 they did their own study, and 簫簫 and they reported that in the reply brief and said they would provide the data if you want. The 80 percent figure that they report for plaintiffs in the Second Circuit includes, by the way, a great number of default judgments where fees are both very small and 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Having practiced in this area, I know you're right.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: So, you know, these statistics can be thrown around, but it is not as if defendants in general are not succeeding in getting attorneys' fees when they're appropriate, but what you 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But 簫簫 but isn't there a problem in having one factor outweigh all others? It's much harder to start a 簫簫 with a presumption up here and all the other factors have to tie against that one to overcome it.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: But it's the fact 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Why don't we just have the Fogerty factors, which are it's one among many?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Your Honor, two 簫簫 two responses. First of all, when people 簫簫 when judges are given discretion to award fees, looking at whether the losing party had a substantial case or not is something that instinctively you arrive at. That's the 簫簫 the standard you 簫簫 you enunciated in Octane for the patent. It's the standard that you enunciated in Martin for the removal statute. Of course that's what you give a lot of primacy to in deciding whether, in your discretion as judges, you're going to shift fees for 簫簫 alter the American Rule or not. That's just natural. It 簫簫 it's what courts have been doing under the Copyright Act since 1909. That is the 簫簫 the law that was reported to Congress when they reenacted this 簫簫 this provision in 1976, in the Brown Study, in the register report. Congress understood that it 簫簫 what the 簫簫 what 簫簫 the way the rule works is that fees are being shifted when one side or the other has an unreasonable litigation 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But since most plaintiffs are not going to sue unless their position is arguably, reasonably present, shouldn't we be looking at how reasonable the defendant's position was?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: You do when they 簫簫 when the plaintiff wins.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: When the defendant wins.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: When the defendant wins, if you have a rule that says we're going to shift fees against reasonable plaintiffs, then you're going to have the wrong incentives. When you have a case where both sides have a reasonable position, what you're trying to do in that case under Fogerty is incentivize both parties to keep litigating so that the law can be clarified. This 簫簫 this is a classic example here. This is a case that was a complete coin flip because the law was totally indeterminant. Nobody knew whether the first簫sale doctrine applied here or didn't apply here. There was no law in the Second Circuit when the case was filed. By the time the case gets to this Court, this Court has already ruled 4簫to簫4 that the 簫簫 on the issue. So nobody 簫簫 it was 簫簫 it was a coin flip. And in that situation, the last thing you want to do to the parties, if you're trying to get them to keep litigating so that they 簫簫 the issue gets clarified is tell them, oh, by the way, we're going to raise the stakes. Whoever wins is going to get fees, and whoever loses is going to have to pay double. That's just not the way risk簫averse, profit簫maximizing participants in litigation behave. If you want 簫簫 you 簫簫 you suppress litigation by imposing the British Rule presumptively in this kind of case because people simply aren't going to keep fighting. They're going to find a way to get out of the case.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: As you understand the Petitioner's position, do they take the argument you've just made about the necessity for continuing the litigation and then add a David and Goliath factor to it? Is that your understanding of their position, or am I 簫簫

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: My understanding of their position 簫簫 and it is a little hard to nail down, Your Honor; it seems to vary at times 簫簫 is from Pages 40 and 41 of the merits brief, the blue brief. And what they say there is if the result of the litigation is to clarify the law, then whichever party has the good fortune of being the winner, wins the coin flip, get's 簫簫 gets fees. That's what they say 簫簫 generally you have to 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: He's departing from that. He's departing from that.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Excuse me?

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: He's departing from that, at least. So you say, just don't say never. And, indeed, the Second Circuit did say never. It said a court should not award attorneys' fees where the case is novel or close because such a litigation clarifies the boundaries of copyright law.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Certainly did not say never, Your Honor. The rule in the Second Circuit is that other factors can overrule that, and indeed, the court has 簫簫 the Second Circuit has so said. They have 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: The way they wrote this here, and they were quoting, it says, "As this Court has reasonably explained." Am I in the right place? I'm in Page 18a, 19a of the 簫簫 of the petition. Maybe I'm reading the wrong petition; that's been known. That's 簫簫 and what they do is they quote. It says, "As this Court recently explained." And it seems to me they underlined the word "not," so I may not have read it properly. I 簫簫 I don't know.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: But, Your Honor 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Anyway, your position is it shouldn't be never.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: And that is the law 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: And maybe everybody agrees.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: That is the law in the Second Circuit. The 簫簫 the Viva Video case, the 簫簫 the Zalewski case both cited 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Smith, when you have a 簫簫 a system, which the Second Circuit does, of saying, look, this is the first among equal factors, and you need something, you know, pretty exceptional to outweigh this factor, if I'm a district judge and I'm thinking, you know, who likes to be overruled by the Second Circuit, it does seem as though it sends a pretty strong signal to district courts that this is the key factor and that they are not 簫簫 you know, probably not in their lifetimes going to see a case in which that factor is outweighed.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: The fact that 簫簫 that can outweigh it, and has outweighed it, is litigation misconduct. So you end up in the Second Circuit with a rule very much like the rule that this Court enunciated for 簫簫 for patent law, which is if the case is unreasonable on either side or if there's been something that has magnified the cost of litigation through litigation abuse, those are situations in which courts appropriately, in their discretion, should award fees. But if we have two reasonable parties litigating appropriately and we don't. They 簫簫 they simply don't know who is going to win because the law is unclear, we don't want to raise the stakes in that situation because those are the people you want to keep fighting. And this case is a perfect illustration. At the point where this case is going to go to this Court, you've already divided 4簫to簫4. Nobody knows where Justice Kagan is going to come out on the issue. First 簫簫 Kirtsaeng has to decide whether to file that cert petition. He knows at that point that he's going to get free representation. But if you had Petitioner's rule in effect, he would also be 簫簫 know that he had a 50 percent chance of losing in this Court and having to pay all of John Wiley's attorney' fees.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: You mentioned that he's getting free representation. Do you 簫簫 you've mentioned the fact that he was represented by pro bono counsel. Is that a factor that the Court should take into consideration?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: I think it can affect the 簫簫 some of the other factors that are relevant under Fogerty, in particular the means 簫簫

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, it seems 簫簫

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: 簫簫 of compensation. You don't need to compensate Mr. Kirtsaeng for representation that he didn't pay for.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, it seems to me that's quite an intrusion into the relationship between the 簫簫 the party and 簫簫 and counsel. I mean, do you look at it and say, oh, well, you have discovery on 簫簫 about whether 簫簫 what the relationship was between him and his counsel 簫簫

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: I think it's 簫簫

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: 簫簫 with the counsel giving a discount on fees and all that? I 簫簫 I'm not sure that should be a pertinent consideration.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Perhaps not. It's 簫簫 it didn't have any major impact on the outcome here. But it certainly was something that the district court mentioned. And, in fact, the Second Circuit kind of disagreed with them on that in a 簫簫 in a footnote in their opinion. But the 簫簫 the main thing is that you don't want to have a rule that says if you file that cert petition, Mr. Kirtsaeng, you're going to be 簫簫 50 percent chance you're going to have a big bill at the end of it. And even from the point of view of John Wiley. Sure 簫簫 sure, it's a big company, and it's a repeat player and everything, but look where they were. Once Mr. Kirtsaeng files that cert petition, if Petitioner's rule is in place, they know they have a 50 percent chance of losing in the Supreme Court and having to pay not only all their lawyers' fees, but all of Mr. Kirtsaeng 簫簫

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: I mean, are you seriously suggesting it's a tough call for them whether to oppose the cert petition or not?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Well, maybe not. But I think it's important to look at the economic situation. They had a $600,000 judgment, and they would have known, under his rule, they had a 50 percent of paying 簫簫

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, what are 簫簫 what are the annual 簫簫

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: 簫簫 several million dollars in fees.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: What are the annual revenues that you're 簫簫

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Mr. Rosenkranz said 1.8 billion. Maybe that's correct. I don't know, actually, Your Honor. But it is 簫簫 it is certainly a substantial publishing outfit, and it's a repeat player and had incentives to litigate this case. But I don't think you can do these things sort of after the fact on 簫簫 based on each party's particular incentives. You need a rule that says to people, here's how we're going to decide these things so you have predictability, so people can tell whether or not this is likely to be 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The problem with your situation is that you're looking at incentives. If I'm Wiley, I'm looking at this and saying, there's a 90 percent chance 簫簫 80 to 90 percent chance I'm going to get fees, because willful infringers 簫簫 and that's the judgment that was being defended below 簫簫 almost always, the winning plaintiff gets fees. If I'm a defendant, I'm Kirtsaeng, I know that the probability is 簫簫 taking Mr. Rosenkranz's numbers, are that maybe 50 percent of the prevailing defendants gets fees. If without pro bono counsel, do you think he would have continued?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: I 簫簫 I assume he probably 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And would have gone into debt for it? I 簫簫 I don't know the answer, but the 簫簫 the incentives are very, very different for a defendant who's being asked to go on with the litigation because the likelihood of them getting fees is so much smaller.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: I think there was never any significant likelihood of anybody getting fees under a proper application of the Second Circuit's rule here because, obviously, both sides had objectively reasonable positions. And I just don't think that that is something that would have happened in 簫簫 in this.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: He got a willfulness found below.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Well, but 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: How do you get a willfulness found in a situation where you know there's a circuit 簫簫

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: But 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: 簫簫 where there is a chance of a split court, 4簫to簫4?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: But the 簫簫 the conduct was 簫簫 was intentional, but the law was 50/50. There was 簫簫 was a complete coin flip. And 簫簫 and so it seems to me that while he says, we don't want to pretermit discretion here, the reality is you have to have some structure to the decision making so people can predict what the outcome's going to be and be incentivized. Otherwise, you just have a black box. You say, well, each district judge takes six factors. We're not going to really tell you how you to decide them. And then you're not doing what Fogerty asks. What Fogerty asks is use the fee decision whether to award fees, how much, to incentivize people to clarify the law because of the peculiar importance of copyright having the 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Are you asking us, in our decision, to endorse the Second Circuit test and reject the Ninth Circuit test, articulate it the way that the Ninth Circuit does, what 簫簫 the Second Circuit does? Ask us exactly what you want us to announce with respect to what the test should be or not be.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: We think the Second Circuit's test makes eminent sense and ought to be upheld. I would note, though, that since all of the factors here went the same way in the judgment of the district court, even if you decided that they shouldn't be giving substantial weight to one factor, there wouldn't be a basis for a reversal in this 簫簫 this particular case. But we do think that starting with the objective reasonableness makes a lot of sense. It leads to the right outcome and the right incentives, and it gives people some basis for being able to figure out what's going to happen in the case and decide which cases to litigate to the end and which cases to settle or simply to abandon.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: How do you answer the argument that you 簫簫 you referred to patent cases, but the patent statute says "fees" in exceptional cases.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: The Copyright Act doesn't say that.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: And I think that is, in fact, how it's played out. The fees are much more rare in patent cases than they are in copyright cases. It's certainly not 簫簫 not true that they're exceptional in 簫簫 in copyright cases. Quite the opposite. It's clearly a large majority of cases are 簫簫 are having fees awarded. It 簫簫 the point, though, is even if you 簫簫 if you layer on that exceptionality requirement, the factors that you look at, was it badly litigated, should it not have been litigated, was there a reasonable basis, was there abusive conduct, that's the factors you should look at. That's what courts have always looked at. Why would you look at something else? I mean, that is the reason why one awards fees is either because somebody brought a case they shouldn't have, or litigated a defense they shouldn't have, or abused the process and 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Do you have a view, Mr. Smith, as to why it is that this reasonableness inquiry is producing such skewed results as to plaintiffs and defendants? Because as a logical matter, you would think it shouldn't, that 簫簫 that there 簫簫 there wouldn't be this skew.

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: I mean 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Unless you really think the defendants are taking so many more unreasonable litigating positions. And I 簫簫 I guess that could be. But is there any other explanation or any, you know, thoughts you have about that?

turns.name: MR. SMITH
turns.text: Part 簫簫 part of it is the default judgments where fees are routinely awarded because there is nobody there to oppose them in 簫簫 in very small amounts. But I 簫簫 I think the other thing is, as the government points out in their brief, plaintiffs decide when to bring cases, defendants don't decide when to be defendants, and there are a lot of intentional infringers of copyrights out there in the world that we have now. And so people 簫簫 it would be bizarre in a way if plaintiffs didn't have a higher percentage of claims that were reasonable, because they decide what case to bring, and they know that they're going to be spending money, and this is 簫簫 they're going to invest in this case, and they 簫簫 they decide to go ahead. Whereas defendants are simply often just caught. You know, they 簫簫 they were hoping to just slide under the radar screen and put these infringing photographs up on their website or whatever it may be. And so it 簫簫 it doesn't strike 簫簫 strike me as surprising at all, actually, that we have this disparity. It doesn't mean that the standard is unfair or is 簫簫 is anything else less than even handed. It simply means that the facts on the ground are leading to a 簫簫 a difference in the outcome in percentages, which we have a lot of different percentages here. But, you know, a lot of defendants are getting fees too, even in the Second Circuit, according to their own statistics, half the time. So it's not like it's entirely one簫sided. If the Court has no more questions.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Ms. Goldenberg. FOR UNITED STATES, AS AMICUS CURIAE, SUPPORTING THE RESPONDENT

turns.name: MS. GOLDENBERG
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: I'd like to start by picking up on this point that's gotten a fair bit of discussion about what the statistics are in the Second Circuit under the Matthew Bender standard which says that objective reasonableness should be given substantial weight. Looking at hundreds and hundreds of cases in the Second Circuit, in the district courts, I think that there is a lot of discretion that one can exercise in deciding whether to include cases in your count or not include cases in your count, whether you include default judgments, whether you include declaratory judgment situations, whether you include situations where the Court says as a technical matter, you can't get fees because your motion was late. But in the alternative, if I were to consider it, I would go on to award or not award you fees, anyway. So I think that there is some ground to quibble with some of the statistics, and I can tell you what statistics I came up when I did this look, which are a little bit different, and I think show that there is not this vast disparity. And the statistics 簫簫 this is in the district courts in the Second Circuit from Matthew Bender on 簫簫 show that including default judgments in the count, 77 percent of the time when plaintiffs ask for fees and it was decided on the merits in a reported decision, they got fees, and 53 percent of the time when defendants asked for fees. But if you drop out those default judgments, which, as Mr. Smith indicated, are situations where the fee motion is effectively unopposed, where I think the defendant often looks very unreasonable by not having shown up to defend the case, and where the fee amount is quite small by necessity because not much has happened in the case, then the numbers start to look more similar, 59.7 percent for plaintiffs, 53 percent for defendants. So there is not this huge gulf between the percentages of time when plaintiffs and defendants are getting fees, at least by my count. And as I say, I recognize there are different ways to do this count. And so I think you have to take all of these numbers with a little bit of a grain of salt. But I don't think any standard in the world would give you equal numbers of plaintiffs and defendants getting attorneys' fees. The Matthew Bender test is neutral. It's even簫handed on its face. It says very clearly that plaintiffs and defendants should be treated in the same way. And if the Court thought that that somehow weren't being carried out properly in the Second Circuit, I think the Court could emphasize that, and it wouldn't be a reason to reject the Second Circuit's test. With respect to giving objective reasonableness substantial weight, I would like to point out that that is the approach that this Court took in the Martin decision, which was a case about removal and remand and which involved 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I'm sorry. Which case?

turns.name: MS. GOLDENBERG
turns.text: Martin v. Franklin, which was a case that involved the statute that, much like the statute here, just gave broad discretion to district courts without a lot of standards to guide them. And what the Court said in that case is discretion isn't whim. In order for like cases to be treated alike, district court's discretion should be guided in certain ways so that there can be predictability and so that there can be 簫簫 that principle of justice can be upheld, even in the absence of expressed statutory restriction.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: You didn't have the sort of situation you have here, where you're concerned about encouraging people to move for remand or discouraging people from filing for fees under remand. It was 簫簫 the policy sort of pushed all one way in Martin.

turns.name: MS. GOLDENBERG
turns.text: Well, it's true that only one side in Martin could get fees. That's true that 簫簫 that 簫簫 it's the 簫簫 when the case is remanded, so the person has been unsuccessful in removing, that's when fees are awarded. But nevertheless, like any fee簫shifting statute, it is taking into account incentives on both sides, whether you should try to remove the case, whether you should move to remand the case if you're on the other side. And what happens in the copyright world because of this Court's Fogerty decision is that those incentives are judged as to plaintiffs and defendants because both of them can get fees, but I don't think the underlying policies are different. In Martin, the Court wasn't looking at anything that was specific to that statute, as to its history, or to any policy that was specific to that statute at all. But what the Court said was that objective reasonableness was the touchstone. And, yes, it may be true that in some cases where the losing party has been objectively reasonable, fees are appropriate, in any event, because this is an equitable matter, and we don't want to restrict the district court's discretion, and it's hard to imagine every single case that could possibly come up in the future. And that is equivalent to what the Second Circuit has done here. So it's very consistent with the approach that the Court has taken to other fee簫shifting statutes where there is broad discretion.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Smith suggested that he thought the times in which the reasonableness inquiry would be outweighed is if you see real litigation misconduct. Is that your sense too, or is there anything else that actually is capable of outweighing it?

turns.name: MS. GOLDENBERG
turns.text: My sense is that that is probably going to be the most frequent circumstance in which it would be outweighed, and there are, as Mr. Smith pointed out, some cases in the Second Circuit like that, be the Video, Zalewski cases, where even though the losing party was objectively reasonable, the Court said there has been some misconduct here and so awarded fees may be appropriate, or the district court should go back and see if there was misconduct here.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Maybe 簫簫

turns.name: MS. GOLDENBERG
turns.text: So there are other examples as well that I'd like to point out beyond that. And one example comes from the 簫簫 the Sixth Circuit, and their case is called WB Music and Bridgeport Music, and that was a situation where plaintiffs indiscriminately brought hundreds and hundreds of claims, some of which were objectively reasonable, and some of which were not objectively reasonably. And what the Court said there was, yes, it's true that your claim was objectively reasonable, even though you didn't succeed, but you've proceeded in this unreasonable fashion. And so there's a strong deterrence factor that's playing in here. We don't want people to do this. We want to stop people from doing this in the future. And so we're going to award attorneys' fees in that situation. That is related, I think, to litigation misconduct, but it's not exactly the same thing. And it is a deterrence. It's focusing on the deterrence factor in the Fogerty footnote. There is another example from the district courts in the Second Circuit. It's a case called Tips Exports. It's a Eastern District of New York case, and that's a case where the defendant lost. And what the court said again was that there is the deterrence factor that comes in here. The defendant was reasonable 簫簫 objectively reasonable in its position in its 簫簫 on the facts and the law, but it appears that the defendant is going to take this just as a cost of doing business and keep on engaging in infringing conduct about because the award of fees isn't enough 簫簫 I'm sorry 簫簫 the award of damages is not enough to stop it. So in that situation, again, deterrence will override the fact that the losing party was objectively reasonable. So I do think it 簫簫

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Counsel, it 簫簫 is it pertinent, in the government's view, whether or not the party seeking fees was represented by pro bono counsel?

turns.name: MS. GOLDENBERG
turns.text: I think it would be pertinent if you were to adopt an approach like Petitioner's approach where you took financial condition of the parties into account. If you are going to do that, then I think you would certainly need to look to see whether somebody who appeared to be an impecunious party was actually represented pro bono, was not responsible for their fees. But I take some issue with what Petitioner's counsel said, that 簫簫 that the financial condition of the parties is something that courts look to when they're deciding whether to make a fee award in the first incidence. I'm not aware of any other circumstance where the courts look to the financial condition of the parties under a fee簫shifting statute to decide whether to award fees on a granular level; in other words, they look to the specific finances of the specific party before them. They certainly look at it when it comes time to decide what the amount of a fee award should be, if they've already decided that they are going to award fees, and that, I think, is perfectly appropriate. And you'll see district courts in the Second Circuit, under the Matthew Bender standard, doing exactly that. If they have a very unreasonable pro se plaintiff, for instance, they will say that a fee award may still be appropriate if that party loses, but perhaps the amount should be set lower, it will still be a deterrent to that person, but it won't be financially crushing to them.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Fogerty says 簫簫 it adds, "The need in particular circumstances to advance considerations of compensation and deterrence."

turns.name: MS. GOLDENBERG
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: It lists the reasonable fact 簫簫 you know, reasonable position is one among four. It says there could be others. Why? Why not stop right there?

turns.name: MS. GOLDENBERG
turns.text: First 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Maybe Marbury was a poor man. Maybe he didn't even want the job. Maybe he was just trying to try to create a situation where this country would have a structure of judicial review. I mean, can they take things like that? Why not?

turns.name: MS. GOLDENBERG
turns.text: Well, it 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: I mean, I 簫簫 I don't know. It has to be consistent with the Act.

turns.name: MS. GOLDENBERG
turns.text: I 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Why are we suddenly picking this one thing out of what could be a bunch of things?

turns.name: MS. GOLDENBERG
turns.text: For a number of reasons. First of all, if you look at the factors that are mentioned in Fogerty, they actually, many of them, center around objective reasonableness: Frivolousness, deterrence, motivation. All of those are kind of circling around this concept of objective reasonableness, which, as I pointed out, is pretty common to the Court's approach to other fee簫shifting statutes, not only in Martin, but also as Mr. Smith explained, in Octane. So that 簫簫 that's one example. There's another example 簫簫 another reason, though, that's very grounded in the Copyright Act itself, and that is the history of the Copyright Act and the ratification that Congress engaged in in 1976 when it chose to readopt essentially the same language from the 1909 Copyright Act as to which courts have, through exercise of their discretion over many years, worn a groove that said, generally, when the losing party is reasonable, fees are not going to be appropriate. That's equivalent to the Second Circuit's standard. Congress had every reason to know that that was the law under the existing language because Congress was presented, by experts, by the register of copyrights, whose job it is to advise Congress on copyright policy, by the Brown Study, which was an expert Copyright Office study that Congress had commissioned. All these authorities said that, and so Congress had good reason to know that it was true.

turns.name: JUSTICE KAGAN
turns.text: If you were thinking of this solely as a policy matter, if you didn't think that that evidence was all that overwhelming, why is it that this factor should be first among equals?

turns.name: MS. GOLDENBERG
turns.text: For the 簫簫 I think a lot of the reasons that Mr. Smith explained with respect to incentives. When you make that factor important, you're encouraging reasonable arguments, you're discouraging unreasonable arguments, you're increasing the chances that close cases where both sides are reasonable are going to actually get litigated to their conclusion, and therefore, the law of copyright will be clarified, which is what this Court called for in its Fogerty decision. If, on the other hand, you adopt something like the standard that Petitioner at least set forth in his brief, where you privilege the precedent簫setting nature of the decision, then you have the tremendous unpredictability, tremendous uncertainty, and risk簫averse parties are going to be deterred, and they won't litigate those close cases to the end, and the law will not be clarified.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Five minutes, Mr. Rosenkranz.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor. So Justice Ginsburg's question, which got picked up through the course of the argument, I think really gets to the nub of the matter. The magic language in Mr. Smith's presentation is that the Second Circuit has adopted the Patent Act standard. The Patent Act standard is different. And if Octane means anything, it is we read the words that Congress actually wrote. "Exceptional circumstances" this Court defined in exactly the way that the government and Wiley are defining this standard. We have to take Congress at its word that it meant something different.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: But it does mean something different in practice because, as we were just told, it's unusual. There are not many fee awards made in patent cases, much higher number in copyright cases.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. The 簫簫 and 簫簫 and that's because in every circuit but the Second Circuit, the 簫簫 the district courts apply a different standard from the Second Circuit standard. That's why you have 簫簫

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: We have the 簫簫 the Second Circuit, and what was it, 50 percent of the defendants and 70 something percent, whatever, it's a much higher percentage than in 簫簫 than in patent cases.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Agreed, Your Honor. And it's important to understand why. Our numbers are 44 percent and 85 percent. One can quibble about the numbers. But I have to emphasize, neither Mr. Smith nor the government has come forward with a single case in which a defendant got fees where the plaintiff was not being reasonable. In every single one of their cases in the Second Circuit, when a defendant got fees, it's because the plaintiff's position was illegal 簫簫 or was unreasonable or the plaintiff took unreasonable positions within the litigation. There is not a single case in the Second Circuit where anything other than unreasonableness carried the day. I want to make 簫簫 I want to say a word about history because there's been a lot of suggestion that the history before 1976 and the Copyright Act was an exceptional簫circumstances standard. It wasn't. There is not a single case that the government or 簫簫 or Wiley has cited, not one case that adopts the Matthew Bender standard across the board. So sure 簫簫 that is pre簫1976 I'm talking about. So sure, there were a lot of cases where unreasonable plaintiffs or defendants were hit with fees. There were cases where the reasonableness of a position for the district court carried the day, but not one case that ever said, here's how we should figure this out. All of the studies that the government has referred to are studies that admitted that there was actually no one standard. This Court has quoted the 簫簫 I'm sorry 簫簫 this Court in Fogerty underscored that there was no standard that predated 1976. And even the cases that Wiley cites, half of them are cases where one of the parties was unreasonable. That leaves only four cases, and those are all cases that are consistent with what we're saying the rule was. Final point: If we're talking about incentives, the difference between my position and Mr. Smith's position is that he wants a rule that decides up front for all district courts that this is the weight you will put on something, and I want a rule, consistent with Congress's language and the use of the word "may," that trusts district courts to figure out what the right incentives and disincentives are, and to figure out what the value is to put on reasonableness.

turns.name: JUSTICE ALITO
turns.text: That's an awfully hard task for district judges to perform, whether 簫簫 you know, what is 簫簫 what will further the purposes of the Copyright Act or what is the most faithful to the Copyright Act. District court judges are going to see that very differently, and there won't be any consistency if that's what they are required to do or authorized to do.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Your Honor, what will emerge is what's emerged in the Ninth Circuit, a common law of equity where courts are following each other's decisions. And, yes, there will be some variability, but the variability is invited by Congress in the word "may." Unreasonable litigants will always be hit with attorneys' fees, not because there is any particular weight put on it by a court of appeals, but because that's what district courts will do. But reasonable positions should be hit with attorneys' fees or not depending upon whether the Court believes that the public was benefitted by the litigation position.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: If there are no further questions.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel.

turns.name: MR. ROSENKRANZ
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: The case is submitted.
